aliases: BBQ pork and shrimp fried rice

english: BBQ pork and shrimp fried rice

region_of_origin: China (Yangzhou, Jiangsu Province); widely adopted in Singapore hawker culture

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific Yang Chow / Yangzhou fried rice entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried rice as a composite dish. No Yangzhou-specific sub-code exists; closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Yang Chow fried rice or fried rice as a food product found in publicly available SNOMED CT browser as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker serving of Yang Chow fried rice is approximately 300 g (one plate or bowl), which is typical for a single-person meal at a Singapore hawker centre or coffee shop.

nutrient_source.title: Yang Chow Fried Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yang Chow Fried Rice (cr_id F05100152). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried rice with meat and shrimp (NDB 22904) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice-based fried rice composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Yang Chow fried rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 is assigned based on the closest analogous entry: white rice-based fried rice (GI ~72–80, mean ~75) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables and corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database entries for white rice composite dishes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 75.87) / 100 = 56.9, using 300 g serving size with 25.29 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 354 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 1,062 mg sodium, representing 47% of the 2,300 mg daily limit, with minimal potassium or dietary fibre offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Yang Chow fried rice to a half-portion (≤150 g) and request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce; char siu should be minimised or omitted as it contributes additional sodium and saturated fat.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (GI 75) combined with 25.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 0.36 g dietary fibre per 100 g results in rapid postprandial glucose elevation; a 300 g serving carries a glycaemic load of approximately 57.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict portion size to 150 g or fewer, pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt glycaemic response, and consider requesting brown rice or cauliflower rice substitution to lower the GI and increase fibre content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 1.8 g per 100 g (5.4 g per 300 g serving) from char siu and cooking oil; sodium at 354 mg per 100 g further elevates cardiovascular risk; high-heat wok frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit serving size to 150–200 g, request less char siu and reduced sauce, and balance the meal with omega-3-rich foods and high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Char siu (BBQ pork) is a red meat source of arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory pathways; high-heat wok frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; dietary fibre is only 0.36 g per 100 g with no meaningful anti-inflammatory offset.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request substitution of char siu with chicken breast or tofu, reduce portion size, and supplement the meal with anti-inflammatory foods such as leafy greens or omega-3-rich fish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yang Chow Fried Rice (扬州炒饭, Yángzhōu Chǎofàn) is a classic Chinese fried rice dish originating from Yangzhou in Jiangsu Province, China, and widely served across Singapore hawker centres, coffee shops, and school canteens. It is prepared by stir-frying day-old cooked white rice in a very hot wok with vegetable oil, scrambled eggs, char siu (Cantonese BBQ pork), shrimp, green peas, diced carrots, and spring onions, seasoned with soy sauce and oyster sauce. The result is a savoury, slightly smoky dish with a tender yet slightly chewy texture from the rice, contrasting with the sweetness of char siu, the bite of shrimp, and the freshness of peas and carrots. Per 100 g it provides 198 kcal, 7.4 g protein, 7.43 g fat, and 25.3 g carbohydrate with only 0.36 g dietary fibre, reflecting a refined white rice base. Sodium is 354 mg per 100 g, making a standard 300 g plate a high-sodium meal (~1,062 mg). The glycaemic index is high (~75), and the glycaemic load per standard serving is approximately 57, making this dish a significant postprandial glucose challenge. It is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis without portion control and ingredient modification. The dish is not halal-certified in its traditional form due to char siu pork, and contains allergens including crustaceans, eggs, soy, and gluten.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100152) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried rice with meat and shrimp entry. GI assigned as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables for white rice-based fried rice. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. sugar_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source entry — the dish contains no added sugar beyond trace amounts in oyster sauce and char siu glaze that fall below the reporting threshold in the HPB database. 2. saturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g) is an adapted estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried rice with meat and shrimp (NDB 22904), as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific Yang Chow fried rice preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F05100152, and no sufficiently close composite entry exists in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy to provide defensible adapted values for all these fields. 4. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Yang Chow fried rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 5. The dish is not halal in its traditional form as it contains char siu (pork). 6. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as GL = (75 × 25.29 × 300/100) / 100 = (75 × 75.87) / 100 = 56.9 for a 300 g serving.
